symbols: crucifix — the focus of Jerome's devotion and the source of salvation
skull — memento mori, the inevitability of death and the need for repentance
books and papers — Jerome's scholarly work translating the Bible (Vulgate)
bare chest — ascetic self-denial and penitence
upward gaze — communion with Christ on the cross
description: A half-length figure of an emaciated elderly man with a long grey beard, bare-chested, seated at a table in deep shadow. He gazes upward to the left where a small crucifix stands. On the table before him lie papers, books, and a string of rosary beads. A human skull rests beside the crucifix. His right hand is pressed to his chest in a gesture of devotion or penitence. A reddish cloth partially drapes his left shoulder. The dramatic tenebristic lighting illuminates only the figure and the objects on the table, with the rest lost in darkness.
theological_description: Matthias Stom depicts Saint Jerome (c. 342-420) in his characteristic dual role as penitent and scholar. Jerome is best known for translating the Bible into Latin (the Vulgate), making Scripture accessible to the Western world for over a millennium. The books and papers reference this monumental scholarly achievement — Jerome took seriously Paul's admonition: 'Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth' (2 Tim 2:15). The crucifix toward which he gazes is the center of his faith, as Paul declared: 'I decided to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified' (1 Cor 2:2). The skull is the classic memento mori, reminding of Genesis 3:19 — 'you are dust, and to dust you shall return.' Jerome's ascetic, bare-chested appearance reflects his years as a hermit in the desert near Bethlehem, where he combined rigorous self-denial with intellectual labor. His posture of devotion echoes the Psalmist: 'As a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God' (Ps 42:1).

scripture_refs.reason: Rightly handling the word of truth — Jerome's life work of Bible translation
I decided to know nothing except Christ crucified — Jerome gazing at the crucifix
You are dust and to dust you shall return — the skull as memento mori
My soul pants for God — Jerome's intense devotional gaze
